titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Willis Photographs of Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota
Willis (John) Photographs of Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota
titlestmt.author: Matthew Daniel Mason and Jennifer Garcia

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday August 21, 2018 at 21:02

did.unittitle: Photographs of Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Inkjet prints of photographs by John Willis that document sites and persons in Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota chiefly in 2013.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints of photographs by John Willis that document sites and persons in Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota chiefly in 2013. Most of the 218 inkjet prints are single images with small groups of diptychs and triptychs.

Photographs of Arizona include views of communities at Navajo Nation including Chinle, Kayenta, and Tuba City, as well as discrete groups of images in Window Rock of the Navajo Military Graveyard and "wheatpaste" photographic poster murals by physician photographer James Edward Thomas, also known as Chip Thomas and "jetsonorama." Other images include views of the Navajo Generating Station, a coal-fired powerplant located near Page, as well as views of Glen Canyon Dam and Lake Powell.

Photographs of Nebraska include views of Whiteclay as well as "Camp Zero Tolerance," a protest camp established in April-June 2013 at the border of Nebraska and the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation that sought to stop alcohol sales in Whiteclay to reservation residents. Informal portraits of protesters include Bryan V. Brewer, the Oglala Sioux Tribal President, as well as camp spokeswoman Tonia Stands.
Photographs of New Mexico include views of landscapes in Edgewood.

Photographs of North Dakota include views of the derelict San Haven Sanitarium owned by the Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa Indians of North Dakota as well as a small group of images of the aftermath of a trailer home fire at the Turtle Mountain Indian Reservation. A group of images documents the oil boom in Williston and the housing for petroleum industry workers.

Photographs of the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation include views of communities comprising Allen, Batesland, Kyle, Manderson, Pine Ridge. Porcupine, Potato Creek, and Sharps Corner. Sites documented include staff housing for the Indian Health Service Pine Ridge Hospital, public housing at Potato Creek, sun dance grounds at Porcupine owned by Warfield Moose, and discrete images of the homes of the Dullknife and Morrison families in Potato Creek. Events documented include a memorial service for Alma Richard in 2011 and a fireworks celebration by the Oglala Sioux Tribe for the Fourth of July in 2013. Portraits of individuals include Mercedes Little Spotted Horse, Santana Little Spotted Horse, Andrea Reddest Marshall, Warfield Moose, Wilbur Morrison, and Jennifer Yellow Bull.

Photographs of South Dakota include views of the Black Hills as well as the communities of Interior, Oglala, and Scenic. Images of the Black Hills include Black Elk Peak (formerly Harney Peak) and Mount Rushmore National Memorial as well as portraits of Kagaba shamans visiting the area from the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia. Images also document a Fourth of July rodeo at Interior and a portrait of Quincy Red Feather with another man.
Signed, titled, and dated by the photographer on versos of prints.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John Willis on the Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2013, and gift of John Willis, 2013.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into nine series: I. Arizona, 2013. II. Nebraska and Camp Zero Tolerance, 2013. III. New Mexico, 2013. IV. North Dakota, 2013. V. Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, 2013. VI. South Dakota, 2011-2013. VII. Diptychs, 2013. VIII. Triptychs, 2013. IX. Oversize Inkjet Prints.

bioghist.head: John Willis (born 1957)
bioghist.p: John Willis is a documentary photographer and instructor of photography at Marlboro College in Marlboro, Vermont. His monographs include Recycled Realities , co-authored with Tom Young (Center for American Places, 2002) and Views from the Reservation (Center for American Places, 2010). Willis has photographs in the permanent collections of the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston, and the Museum of Fine Arts in Houston, among others. He is also a co-founder with Bill Ledger of the In-Sight Photography Project, a photography outreach project to youth in Brattleboro, Vermont.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Willis, Photographs of Arizona, Nebraska, New Mexico, North Dakota, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

subject.part: Fourth of July -- South Dakota -- Interior -- Pictorial works
Fourth of July -- South Dakota -- Pine Ridge -- Pictorial works

corpname.part: Pine Ridge Indian Hospital (S.D.) -- Pictorial works

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into two subseries: Navajo Nation, and Glen Canyon Dam and Lake Powell.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under five headings: Chinle, Kayenta, Navajo Generating Station, Tuba City, and Window Rock.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Navajo Generating Station is a coal-fired powerplant located on the Navajo Nation, near Page, Arizona.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One print unsigned by the photographer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Detail views of weathered United States flags at Navajo Veterans' Cemetery.

did.unittitle: Photographic Murals by James Edward Thomas, Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Views of "wheatpaste" photographic poster murals created physician and artist James Edward Thomas, Jr., also known as Dr. Chip Thomas and "jetsonorama."

did.unittitle: Glen Canyon Dam and Lake Powell

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 19 inches (33 centimeters x 48.2 centimeters) .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into two subseries: Whiteclay, Nebraska, and Camp Zero Tolerance.

title.part: Whiteclay, Nebraska, Where Over 11,000 Cans of Beer are Sold a Day in a Town with 24 Full Time Residents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Camp Zero Tolerance was a protest camp established in April-June 2013 at the border of Whiteclay, Nebraska, and the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. The camp sought to stop alcohol sales in Whiteclay to residents of the reservation.

In his descriptions, the photographer used abbreviations, which this guide spells out, as well as correcting occasional misspellings. Some inscriptions refer to "Camp Zero Justice," which represents a misdescription.

title.part: Sign Referencing Liquor Store Owners in Whiteclay, Nebraska
Tonia Stands, Spokeswoman for Camp Zero Tolerance speaks to the Media
Protesting Alcohol Sales in Whiteclay, Nebraska
Camp Zero Justice Protesters Impose Impromptu Alcohol Check Road Block
Oglala Sioux Tribe Tribal Alcohol Road Block Entering Pine Ridge Reservation from Whiteclay, Nebraska (inspired by Camp Zero Tolerance)
Requesting Implementation of Alcohol Road Block
Bryan Brewer, Oglala Sioux Tribal President, Supporting Camp Zero Tolerance
Strategic Planning with Bryan Brewer Tribal President, Camp Zero Tolerance
Whiteclay, Nebraska, next to Camp Zero Tolerance
Looking Toward Whiteclay, Nebraska, from Pine Ridge Reservation
Border of the Pine Ridge Reservation and Whiteclay, Nebraska
Border of the Pine Ridge Reservation with Whiteclay, Nebraska
Tipi, Border of the Pine Ridge Reservation with Whiteclay, Nebraska

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters), of sites in Edgewood, New Mexico.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series IV is organized into four subseries: San Haven Sanitarium, Turtle Mountain Indian Reservation, Williston, and Unidentified Sites.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Views of the derelict San Haven Sanitarium in Dunseith, North Dakota (established as the North Dakota Tuberculosis Sanitarium in 1909 and vacated in 1989).
The Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa Indians of North Dakota purchased the property from the State of North Dakota in 1992.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints chiefly of a trailer home destroyed by fire.

title.part: RV Park for Oil Boom Housing
Roll of Attendance, Small Town Church

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 19 inches (33.0 centimeters x 48.3 centimeters) except where noted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series V is organized into nine subseries: Allen, Batesland, Kyle, Manderson, Pine Ridge, Porcupine, Potato Creek and Vicinity, Sharps Corner, and Unidentified Community.

title.part: Alma Richard's Memorial, Allen Community Action Program (CAP) Building

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet print, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters), of a memorial service for Alma W. Richard (1952-2011).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three inkjet prints, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters), and one inkjet print, 13 inches x 19 inches (33.0 centimeters x 48.3 centimeters).

arrangement.p: Organized under four headings: Indian Health Service Pine Ridge Hospital, Housing, Oglala Sioux Tribe Fireworks, and Sites and Persons.

arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Dullknife Family Home, Morrison Family Home, and Potato Creek Housing followed by a view of the Potato Creek Sundance grounds.

title.part: Selling Traditional Herbs for Ceremonial Use

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, 13 inches x 19 inches (33.0 centimeters x 48.3 centimeters) except where noted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VI is organized into four subseries: Black Hills, Interior, Oglala, and Scenic.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Sites and Kogi Shamans.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Harney Peak was renamed Black Elk Peak in August 2016 in honor of Black Elk (1863-1950), medicine man and holy man of the Oglala Lakota Sioux.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Kogi, also known as Kagaba, are an indigenous group that lives in the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia.

title.part: Kogi Elder Medicine Man Whom Traveled from Columbia to Meet with Lakota Elders
Kogi Elder Medicine Woman Whom Traveled from Columbia to Meet with Lakota Elders
Kogi Medicine Man Whom Traveled from Columbia to Meet with Lakota Elders
Freedom Days, 4th of July Rodeo, Reservation Bordertown
Oglala Lakota College Old Nursing School Dorm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three inkjet prints, 13 inches x 16 inches (33.0 centimeters x 40.6 centimeters).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, each with two images, 11 inches x 21.5 inches (27.9 centimeters x 54.6 centimeters) except where noted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VII is organized into four subseries: Nebraska and Camp Zero Tolerance, Pine Ridge Indian Reservation, and South Dakota.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Whiteclay, Nebraska, and Camp Zero Tolerance.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Camp Zero Tolerance was a protest camp established in April-June 2013 near the border between Nebraska and the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation to shut down alcohol sales in Whiteclay, Nebraska, to residents of the dry reservation.

title.part: Abandoned Building on the Border of Whiteclay, Nebraska, and Camp Zero Tolerance About 50 Yards Away from Pine Ridge Reservation
Assisting an Inebriated Lakota Woman, Camp Zero Tolerance, After she came from Whiteclay, Nebraska, Reporting Having Been Raped There

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under four headings: Batesland, Pine Ridge, Porcupine Sundance Grounds, and Potato Creek and Vicinity.

title.part: Santana and Mercedes Little Spotted Horse with Their Boyfriends, 4th of July Powwow (Each Flag Represents a Specific Lakota Veteran)
Warfield Moose, Yuwipi Man and Sundance Leader, the Day the Sundance Arbor was Being Prepared
Dullknife Family and Visitors and the Dullknife Family Home

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inkjet print with two images, 11 inches x 15 inches (27.9 centimeters x 38 centimeters).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Organized under two headings: Black Hills and Scenic.

title.part: Tourist's Memory Through Photography, Mount Rushmore National Monument

scopecontent.p: Inkjet prints, each with three images, 11 inches x 32 inches (27.9 centimeters x 81.3 centimeters).

Selling Crafts in Exchange for Alcohol. Whiteclay, Nebraska
